text: The surgeon utilizes a three-arm robotic approach with a grasping retractor in the furthest right assistant port, a monopolar cautery in the right working arm, and a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left working arm. The initial dissection begins by opening the superior anterior hilum and identifying level X and level V AP window lymph nodes, which are dissected free. The monopolar cautery dissector is used for fine dissection around the apical anterior divisions of the vein, the pulmonary artery, and the first truncus branch of the pulmonary artery on the left side. The fourth assistant arm retracts the lobe anteriorly, and the posterior hilum is approached with the lung gently retracted anteriorly by the fourth assistant grasping arm, allowing the posterior pleura to be opened.

The surgeon performs initial dissection to remove level ten lymph nodes in the posterior hilum, identifying the inferior vein and level seven lymph nodes. The level seven lymph nodes are excised and sent for frozen section, returning as benign. The surgeon approaches level ten lymph nodes to identify the interlobar pulmonary artery using spatula cautery for fine dissection. The posterior hilar interlobar pulmonary artery is identified, marking the posterior boundary of the major fissure. The anterior fissure is identified, and the fissure between the lingula and lower lobe is divided. The lung is retracted anteriorly, and additional vein dissection reveals the lingular vein as distinct from the apical anterior and posterior segmental veins.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the anterior apical posterior segmental veins and uses a robotic stapler to divide and ligate them. The first truncus or apical anterior branch of the upper lobe pulmonary artery is identified, dissected free, and divided with a vascular load using the stapler. Additional hilar dissection opens the mediastinal pleura, revealing a posterior branch of the pulmonary artery, which is also ligated. The posterior apical and anterior bronchus are identified and freed.

The surgeon divides the bronchus under direct control using the robotic console. Following the division of the bronchus, veins, and artery, the vascular supply to the apical anterior and posterior divisions is isolated. Endocyanin green is infused intravenously, highlighting the intersegmental plane with perfusion visible only to the lingula. The intersegmental plane is marked with pinpoint electrocautery, guiding the resection of non-perfused lung areas. The intersegmental plane is then divided using additional staple fires under robotic control.

The surgeon uses near-infrared fluorescence imaging to confirm resection of non-perfused lung areas while sparing the lingula. The lung is manipulated gently under direct control for precise stapler handling. The resected specimen is placed in a bag and removed through an access incision. The completed resection reveals the tumor within the apical anterior and upper division.
